critique.summary: The article argues against the notion that coding is becoming obsolete due to AI advancements, emphasizing that coding remains essential for mastering complexity through abstraction. It highlights the challenges of precise specifications, especially in complex systems like collaborative text editors, and envisions AI and future AGI as tools to enhance, not replace, coding practices.
mainPoints: English specifications feel precise but are often vague until implemented and tested.
AI assists in converting English descriptions into running code, enabling iterative refinement.
"Vibe coding" creates an illusion of precision but breaks down at scale or complexity.
Collaborative text editors are fundamentally complex and difficult to implement correctly.
Abstraction is crucial for managing complexity in programming, allowing humans to handle more than 7±2 concepts at once.
AI and future AGI will help solve harder abstraction problems, improving code quality rather than making coding obsolete.
Code itself is an important artifact, akin to poetry, and not just a means to produce software.
The idea that coding is dead is misguided, similar to thinking storytelling ended with the printing press.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims about the challenges of precise specifications and the complexity of collaborative text editors are well-supported by known software engineering principles and historical difficulties in real-time collaboration software. The emphasis on abstraction aligns with established cognitive science and programming theory. The perspective on AI and AGI as tools to enhance coding rather than replace it reflects mainstream expert opinion and current technological trends. However, some claims, such as the future capabilities of AGI, remain speculative but are presented as opinions rather than facts. The article does not cite multiple external sources or perspectives explicitly, limiting its journalistic rigor.
considerations: The article does not provide empirical data or references to studies supporting the difficulty of collaborative text editors or the effectiveness of AI in coding.
It lacks perspectives from other experts or counterarguments about AI potentially reducing the need for traditional coding skills.
The timeline and impact of AGI remain speculative and uncertain, which should be acknowledged more explicitly.
The article assumes familiarity with programming concepts and does not define terms like 'vibe coding' or 'functional reactive programming' for a general audience.
Potential ethical and societal implications of AI and AGI in software development are not discussed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on whether coding as a skill or practice is becoming obsolete due to AI advancements. Most commenters agree that coding is evolving rather than dying, with AI raising abstraction levels but not fully replacing the need for precise, human-driven code. Concerns about over-reliance on AI and comprehension debt are also highlighted.

comments.author: idopmstuff
comments.text:
I don't know that people are saying code is dead (or at least the ones who have even a vague understanding of AI's role) - more that humans are moving up a level of abstraction in their inputs. Rather than writing code, they can write specs in English and have AI write the code, much in the same way that humans moved from writing assembly to writing higher-level code.

But of course writing code directly will always maintain the benefit of specificity. If you want to write instructions to a computer that are completely unambiguous, code will always be more useful than English. There are probably a lot of cases where you could write an instruction unambiguously in English, but it'd end up being much longer because English is much less precise than any coding language.

I think we'll see the same in photo and video editing as AI gets better at that. If I need to make a change to a photo, I'll be able to ask a computer, and it'll be able to do it. But if I need the change to be pixel-perfect, it'll be much more efficient to just do it in Photoshop than to describe the change in English.

But much like with photo editing, there'll be a lot of cases where you just don't need a high enough level of specificity to use a coding language. I build tools for myself using AI, and as long as they do what I expect them to do, they're fine. Code's probably not the best, but that just doesn't matter for my case.

(There are of course also issues of code quality, tech debt, etc., but I think that as AI gets better and better over the next few years, it'll be able to write reliable, secure, production-grade code better than humans anyway.)
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced view of AI's role in coding, acknowledging both the benefits and limitations of AI-generated code, making it a balanced and accurate perspective.

comments.author: rvz
comments.text:
From "code" to "no-code" to "vibe coding" and back to "code".

What you are seeing here is that many are attempting to take shortcuts to building production-grade maintainable software with AI and now realizing that they have built their software on terrible architecture only to throw it away, rewriting it with now no-one truly understanding the code or can explain it.

We have a term for that already and it is called "comprehension debt". [0]

With the rise of over-reliance of agents, you will see "engineers" unable to explain technical decisions and will admit to having zero knowledge of what the agent has done.

This is exactly happening to engineers at AWS with Kiro causing outages [1] and now requiring engineers to manually review AI changes [2] (which slows them down even with AI).

[0] https://addyosmani.com/blog/comprehension-debt/

[1] https://www.theguardian.com/technology/2026/feb/20/amazon-cl...

[2] https://www.ft.com/content/7cab4ec7-4712-4137-b602-119a44f77...
comments.analysis: The comment highlights real risks of depending too much on AI without understanding the underlying code, supported by concrete examples, making it a credible caution.

comments.author: soumyaskartha
comments.text:
Every few years something is going to kill code and here we are.
 The job changes, it does not disappear.
comments.analysis: This succinctly captures the historical pattern of technological shifts affecting coding jobs without eliminating them, a reasonable and widely accepted viewpoint.

comments.author: pacman128
comments.text: In a chat bot coding world, how do we ever progress to new technologies? The AI has been trained on numerous people's previous work. If there is no prior art, for say a new language or framework, the AI models will struggle. How will the vast amounts of new training data they require ever be generated if there is not a critical mass of developers?
comments.analysis: This comment raises a valid concern about AI limitations in pioneering new technologies due to lack of existing data, an important consideration for AI's future role.

comments.author: rglover
comments.text:
It's only dead to those who are ignorant to what it takes to build and run real systems that don't tip over all the time (or leak data, embroil you in extortion, etc). That will piss some people off but it's worth considering if you don't want to perma-railroad yourself long-term. Many seem to be so blinded by the glitz, glamour, and dollar signs that they don't realize they're actively destroying their future prospects/reputation by getting all emo about a non-deterministic printer.

Valuable? Yep. World changing? Absolutely. The domain of people who haven't the slightest clue what they're doing? Not unless you enjoy lighting money on fire.
comments.analysis: The comment is somewhat emotional but underscores the importance of expertise beyond AI-generated code, a valid point though expressed with some bias.

comments.author: deadbabe
comments.text:
My problem is that while I know “code” isn’t going away, everyone seems to believe it is, and that’s influencing how we work.

I have not really found anything that shakes these people down to their core. Any argument or example is handwaved away by claims that better use of agents or advanced models will solve these “temporary” setbacks. How do you crack them? Especially upper management.
comments.analysis: This highlights a cultural and management challenge around AI and coding, reflecting real workplace dynamics though less focused on technical accuracy.

comments.author: gedy
comments.text:
When I started my professional life in the 90s, we used Visual J++ (Java) and remember all this damn code it generated to do UIs...

I remember being aghast at all the incomprehensible code and "do not modify" comments - and also at some of the devs who were like "isn't this great?".

I remember bailing out asap to another company where we wrote Java Swing and was so happy we could write UIs directly and a lot less code to understand. I'm feeling the same vibe these days with the "isn't it great?". Not really!
comments.analysis: Provides historical context showing skepticism towards generated code, drawing a parallel to current AI trends; insightful but anecdotal.

comments.author: cratermoon
comments.text:
Yet again we can pull out Edsger W.Dijkstra's 1978 article, "On the foolishness of "natural language programming""

"In order to make machines significantly easier to use, it has been proposed (to try) to design machines that we could instruct in our native tongues. this would, admittedly, make the machines much more complicated, but, it was argued, by letting the machine carry a larger share of the burden, life would become easier for us. It sounds sensible provided you blame the obligation to use a formal symbolism as the source of your difficulties. But is the argument valid? I doubt."
comments.analysis: References a respected authority to caution against over-optimism in natural language programming, adding historical depth to the debate.

comments.author: erichocean
comments.text:
> If you know of any other snippet of code that can master all that complexity as beautifully, I'd love to see it.

Electric Clojure: https://electric.hyperfiddle.net/fiddle/electric-tutorial.tw...
comments.analysis: Offers a concrete example relevant to the discussion, though without elaboration it adds limited insight.

comments.author: developic
comments.analysis: This comment adds no substantive content or insight to the discussion.
